articles.title: About Me
articles.description: As part of my degree requirement, I have been developing my quantitative analysis skills through R and I will be showing my progress in my final year project - a policy report - by producing a regression table. The activities I have done in previous assignments will demonstrate the development of my skills.

articles.title: Text Analysis
articles.contents:
Nat's GV300 Website
Portfolio


Home
About
☰


  
    
      
        
        
        
      
      
    
    
      
  Portfolio

      
  Home


  About

      
  






Text Analysis





What is it?
First, this is the packages that will be needed:
library(stringr)
library(car)
## Warning: package 'car' was built under R version 4.4.2
## Loading required package: carData
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following object is masked from 'package:car':
## 
##     recode
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(quanteda)
## Warning: package 'quanteda' was built under R version 4.4.3
## Package version: 4.3.1
## Unicode version: 15.1
## ICU version: 74.1
## Parallel computing: 4 of 4 threads used.
## See https://quanteda.io for tutorials and examples.
library(readtext)
## Warning: package 'readtext' was built under R version 4.4.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'readtext'
## The following object is masked from 'package:quanteda':
## 
##     texts
library(quanteda.textmodels)
## Warning: package 'quanteda.textmodels' was built under R version 4.4.3
Next, you can see the file I have chosen is from a folder called
“Hansard”. The files I will be using for this text analysis is the House
of Commons debate in regards to the recent scandal surrounding Lord
Mandelson and his affiliation to Jeffrey Epstein. Specifically, it
entails the conversation surrounding the allegations of Mandelson
selling state secrets to Epstein.
filenames <- list.files("Hansard", "\\.txt$") 
After this, I will now be narrowing down all of this debate to find
the information surrounding “documents” as I want to know if the House
of Commons did debate about the documents Mandelson has possibly shared
with Epstein. This can be seen below:
txts <-  readtext(paste0("Hansard",  "/", "*.txt"))[]

str(txts)
## Classes 'readtext' and 'data.frame': 6 obs. of  2 variables:
##  $ doc_id: chr  "Lord Mandelson 2026-02-04.txt" "Lord Mandelson_  Response to Humble Address 2026-03-16.txt" "Lord Mandelson_ Government Response to Humble Address 2026-02-23.txt" "Lord Mandelson_ Government Response to Humble Address Motion 2026-02-12.txt" ...
##  $ text  : chr  "Lord Mandelson\n\nMr Speaker\nI inform the House that I have selected the amendment tabled in the name of the P"| __truncated__ "Lord Mandelson:  Response to Humble Address\n\nMadam Deputy Speaker (Judith Cummins)\nBefore we come to the urg"| __truncated__ "Lord Mandelson: Government Response to Humble Address\n\nMr Speaker\nBefore we come to the statement updating t"| __truncated__ " Lord Mandelson: Government Response to Humble Address Motion\n\n10:43:00\n\nAlex Burghart (Brentwood and Ongar"| __truncated__ ...
txtCorpus <- corpus(txts)
head(summary(txtCorpus))
##                                                                          Text
## 1                                               Lord Mandelson 2026-02-04.txt
## 2                  Lord Mandelson_  Response to Humble Address 2026-03-16.txt
## 3        Lord Mandelson_ Government Response to Humble Address 2026-02-23.txt
## 4 Lord Mandelson_ Government Response to Humble Address Motion 2026-02-12.txt
## 5           Lord Mandelson_ Response to  Humble Address Motion 2026-03-11.txt
## 6                      Peter Mandelson_ Release of Information 2026-03-05.txt
##   Types Tokens Sentences
## 1  5014  62554      2868
## 2  1485   7294       268
## 3  1305   5951       226
## 4  1349   6949       307
## 5  1634   9164       345
## 6   115    187         7
options(width = 30)
documents <- kwic(tokens(txtCorpus), "documents")

View(documents)

            Bio
            Hi, my name is Natalie Annett and I am a 3rd year
            Politics and International Relations student, with a
            specific interest in geopolitics. I enjoy reading in my
            spare time outside of my academic responsibilities and I
            have recently found interest in philosophical books.

articles.title: Portfolio
articles.contents:
##GV300 Progress
This is what I have been doing in my previous assignments. Below you will see that I will load the packages needed for the code, as well as the codes that will be essential for the display of the data visualisation.


knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)


After this, I will demonstrate the code that I needed in order to load the data. In this case the dataset is from the Parliament website and it details voter turnout. The data visualisation I intend to show is the turnout between the different devolved nations.


uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% tolower() %>% str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")


Below here is the code required to form the code to make that data visualisation.


uk1 <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`)
uk2 <- uk1 %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country      = country_name,
    valid_votes  = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate   = electorate
  )
uk3 <- uk2 %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate  = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
uk <- uk3 %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)

overall_mean <- mean(uk$valid_votes)


Here is the code that, using the ggplot2 package, that will create the visualisation demonstrating the turnout for the 4 UK devolved nations.


overall_mean <- mean(uk$valid_votes)

ggplot(uk, aes(x = valid_votes)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "#008080", colour = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_mean, 
             color = "#FF2050", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Sizes by Country",
    subtitle = "Red line shows overall UK mean",
    x = "Number of Valid Votes",
    y = "Count of Constituencies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
